text: robotic-assisted repair of a giant pyrosophageal hiatal hernia in a patient with scoliosis. This is a seventy-five-year-old female patient with three episodes of intractable nausea and emesis with prior history of GERD. Workup included abdominal CT scan which showed large type IV hiatus hernia, partial volvulus of the stomach, herniation of the colon into the chest with partial obstruction. Upper endoscopy showed esophagitis, hiatal hernia, and possible gastric volvulus. Esophageal manometry showed hypomotility disorder.

The surgeon inserts a 12mm trocar supraumbilically to the left of the midline for the robotic camera, followed by the placement of four additional trocars to access the left upper quadrant. The left lobe of the liver is retracted anteriorly with a Nathanson retractor, exposing the hiatus.
The surgeon observes a large parasophageal hernia, with the entire stomach herniated into the posterior mediastinum and rotated, positioning the greater curvature adjacent to the right pleura.

The surgeon gently reduces the herniated stomach and begins dividing the short gastric vessels. The hernia sac is sectioned starting at its junction with the left pleura, extending towards the right side around the rim of the hiatus. The left crest is exposed, and the greater curvature of the stomach is freed, allowing dissection to extend into the posterior mediastinum.
A Penrose drain is positioned in the posterior mediastinum, visible within the surgical field.

The operation focuses on the right side of the esophagus, where the surgeon opens the pars flaccida. The right crest is identified and separated from the esophagus using a combination of blunt dissection and a robotic vessel sealer device, extending from anterior to posterior. The posterior aspect of the esophagus is further dissected, and a Penrose drain is used to encircle and retract the esophagus.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt dissection and a vessel sealer scalpel to carefully dissect the hernia sac from its mediastinal attachments, starting from the right side and reducing it into the abdomen, leaving it at the gastroesophageal junction level.

The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus, extending dissection toward the left side both anteriorly and posteriorly to achieve adequate intra-abdominal length. The aorta is visible, curved into the left chest, with the esophagus oriented similarly due to the patient's scoliosis. The esophagus is carefully mobilized away from the aorta by removing areolar attachments, straightening it to gain additional length.

The surgeon begins closure of the diaphragmatic defect after confirming the gastroesophageal junction is positioned well within the abdomen. Five interrupted O-silk sutures are used for primary closure, followed by reinforcement with a U-shaped Bio-A mesh. The mesh is secured to the edges of the right and left crura using two O-soaked sutures.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to resect the hernia sac, carefully removing it from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs a Nissen fundoplication using a 56 French bougie as a guide. Three 2-0 silk stitches are used to create the wrap, with the most distal stitch securing the wrap to the esophagus.
An upper endoscopy was performed at the end of the case. Operative time was a hundred and seventy-three minutes. Estimated blood loss was twenty cc's. Length of stay was two days. Complications none.
